elements.sent-2411-001: The certification of autonomous systems is an important concern in science and industry.
elements.sent-2411-002: The KI-LOK project explores new methods for certifying and safely integrating AI components into autonomous trains.
elements.sent-2411-003: We pursued a two-layered approach: (1) ensuring the safety of the steering system by formal analysis using the B method, and (2) improving the reliability of the perception system with a runtime certificate checker.
elements.sent-2411-004: This work links both strategies within a demonstrator that runs simulations on the formal model, controlled by the real AI output and the real certificate checker.
elements.sent-2411-005: The demonstrator is integrated into the validation tool \prob{ .}
elements.sent-2411-006: This enables runtime monitoring, runtime verification, and statistical validation of formal safety properties using a formal B model.
elements.sent-2411-007: Consequently, one can detect and analyse potential vulnerabilities and weaknesses of the AI and the certificate checker.
elements.sent-2411-008: We apply these techniques to a signal detection case study and present our findings.
elements.sent-2411-009: Artificial intelligence (AI) is increasingly used in safety-critical applications such as autonomous driving~\cite{sun2020scalability and autonomous flying~\cite{nonami2010autonomous,taxinet .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: While AI can be effective for many challenging tasks, it also introduces new risks and concerns.
elements.sent-2411-011: This leads to new challenges regarding certification and ensuring the safety of AI components (see, e.g., Peleska et al.~\cite{peleska2022standardisation in the context of autonomous railway systems).}
elements.sent-2411-012: This work deals with systems that employ an AI perception system, such as image recognition.
elements.sent-2411-013: Those systems include autonomous vehicles and autonomous railway systems.
elements.sent-2411-014: In earlier work~\cite{gruteser2023formal , we formally verified a steering system, assuming the perception system works perfectly.}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, as the perception system is imperfect, we also created simulations with (hand-coded) probabilities for all kinds of erroneous detections.
elements.sent-2411-016: We then applied Monte Carlo simulation to estimate the likelihood of safety-critical errors.
elements.sent-2411-017: As a concrete case study, we applied those techniques to an AI-based railway system~\cite{gruteser2023formal using \prob{ ~\cite{probjournal and \simb{ ~\cite{simb .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we move towards using the {\em real\/ AI within these simulation and validation runs, rather than using estimated error rates.}
elements.sent-2411-019: As outlined by Myllyaho et al.~\cite{literatureValidationAiSystems , fully virtual simulation enables validation of the system in dangerous situations without real danger.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Although the validity of the simulator is difficult to verify, simulation still helps as a validation method to initially assess the quality of the system under evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-021: The AI perception system itself is based on the widely-used YOLO~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/RedmonDGF15 architecture, making the system difficult to verify with formal methods alone.}
elements.sent-2411-022: To address this, we implemented a runtime certificate checker using classical computer vision algorithms to verify the output of the AI~\cite{Rossbach2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This checker can be certified using classical techniques (e.g., \cite{EN50128 ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: This work presents a real-time demonstrator linking the formal model, the AI, and certified control, extending an approach used to validate reinforcement learning agents~\cite{vu2024validation .}
elements.sent-2411-025: AI now controls the simulation directly by executing events for the perception system in the formal model.
elements.sent-2411-026: With our approach, we capture real AI behaviour and can use two runtime monitoring techniques: 1) the formal B model acts as a safety shield for the steering system (e.g., to detect false negatives), 2) and the certified control monitors the perception system (detecting false positives).
elements.sent-2411-027: We demonstrate this methodology on a railway case study~\cite{gruteser2023formal , and discuss our findings and the challenges of the approach.}
elements.sent-2411-028: The B method~\cite{bmethod is a state-based formal method for specifying and verifying software systems.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The B method is based on first-order logic and set theory and has been used industrially for over 25 years \cite{DBLP:conf/fmics/ButlerKKLLMV20 to generate software that is ``correct by construction'' \cite{DBLP:journals/tsi/DolleEF03,Siemens:B2007 , and for system-level safety modelling.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: For the latter, the B method has been used for many railway applications, such as ETCS Hybrid Level 3~\cite{DBLP:journals/sttt/HansenLKKNNSS20,etcs-proof and CBTC systems~\cite{DBLP:conf/rssrail/ComptierDPMTS17,DBLP:conf/rssrail/ComptierLMPM19 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In this article, we use the B method to model autonomous train control in a shunting yard, based on the model from \cite{gruteser2023formal .}
elements.sent-2411-032: It supports various formal languages including the formal B method.
elements.sent-2411-033: We use \prob{ and \simb{ in this article to run the steering system and the safety shield, and also for validating the entire system.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Unlike conventional monitoring methods, certified control does not rely on independent perceptions.
elements.sent-2411-035: Instead, a controller provides a certificate containing all essential information to prove formal properties.
elements.sent-2411-036: This certificate may be generated by a sophisticated AI algorithm, which does not need to be formally verified.
elements.sent-2411-037: Using this certificate, the runtime monitor verifies if the specified criteria hold for the provided data.
elements.sent-2411-038: This monitor can, in contrast to the AI system, be comparatively small and deterministic.
elements.sent-2411-039: The architecture establishes a trusted foundation that can potentially be subjected to a rigorous formal verification process.
elements.sent-2411-040: In previous tests, this technique almost eliminated all false positive detections, in exchange for rejecting some true positives~\cite{Rossbach2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The bounding box of the sign detected by the YOLO model is cropped from the image and passed to the runtime monitor, which uses various computer vision techniques, e.g. contour detection, to validate the sign for expected features.
elements.sent-2411-042: If the desired features are not recognised, the detection is rejected.
elements.sent-2411-043: Figure~\ref{fig:certctrl shows a successfully validated Sh1, and a Sh0 sign that would be rejected by the checker.}
elements.section-2411-002: Linking Formal Model, AI, and Certified Control
elements.sent-2411-044: This section describes how we link together the formal model, the AI, and the certificate checker.
elements.sent-2411-045: In the formal model, we formally specify and verify the steering system which includes safety shields to prevent unsafe operations based on the AI's perception and the known environment.
elements.sent-2411-046: In previous work~\cite{gruteser2023formal on validating an AI-based train control system, we encoded probabilities for false positive and false negative detections of the AI by hand.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Now, we use {\em real\/ AI components and real certificate checkers for simulation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Figure~\ref{fig-overview gives an overview of how we link the formal model, the AI, and certified control inside a real-time demonstrator with runtime monitoring/verification.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In our case study, the AI-based perception system processes the environment in form of images at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-050: Ideally, various techniques should be employed to ensure that the AI is trained correctly and performs well (see \cite{Rossbach2024 and references therein).}
elements.sent-2411-051: Additionally, we use certified control to monitor the perception system, and detect false positives (i.e., the monitor will reject detections which it cannot confirm).
elements.sent-2411-052: The output of the perception system and the certificate checker are then synchronised with the formal model.
elements.sent-2411-053: The simulated environment, including the image provided to the AI, must correspond to the formal model's current state.
elements.sent-2411-054: This is a significant challenge as discussed in Section~\ref{sec:challenges .}
elements.sent-2411-055: The formal model contains events for both the steering system and the perception system.
elements.sent-2411-056: The model should be safe under the assumption that the perception system works perfectly.
elements.sent-2411-057: Furthermore, the formal model can be used as a basis for a safety shield which enforces safe actions on the steering system.
elements.sent-2411-058: The shield can disallow unsafe actions, like driving through a detected stop signal.
elements.sent-2411-059: The safety shield can also detect false negatives.
elements.sent-2411-060: For example, when the AI detects no signals but the formal model ``knows'' that a signal must be visible at the current location, it can enforce a safe fallback action (like stopping).
elements.figure-2411-001: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{fig/OverviewSimulation_v3} \end{center} \caption{Simulation of the Formal B Model with AI-based Perception System and Certificate Checker in an Environment} \label{fig-overview}
elements.sent-2411-061: We apply the presented technique to a case study provided by our project partners (see~\cite{gruteser2023formal ).}
elements.sent-2411-062: For this case study, we developed a formal B model~\cite{gruteser2023formal , consisting of an environment, the steering system, and the perception system.}
elements.sent-2411-063: The environment includes obstacles, points (aka switches) and signal states, field elements and movements of the steered train.
elements.sent-2411-064: The formal model abstracts away the AI-based perception system by events that represent possible outcomes of the object detection, including correct, false positive and false negative detections.
elements.sent-2411-065: The objective, or the ``mission order'', is to drive autonomously from the starting position through a small shunting yard to the destination without dangerous situations or at least as safely as human drivers~(cf.. \cite[PROB1-2]{gruteser2023formal ).}
elements.sent-2411-066: The focus of this work is the detection of signal aspects during the shunting movement.
elements.sent-2411-067: We collected images from multiple videos of the case study track containing the various signal aspects, e.g., stop signals, permission signals, and no signals along the route to capture an interactively changing environment.
elements.sent-2411-068: Based on the position of the train and the state controlled by the formal model, an image with the appropriate signalling aspect is randomly selected from the corresponding collection.
elements.sent-2411-069: For our experiments, we assume that a signal becomes visible as soon as the train is closer than 10 distance units (freely selectable).
elements.sent-2411-070: The procedure for a simulation step is as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-071: Pick an image randomly depending on the current train location and signal states in the B model.
elements.sent-2411-072: Pass the image to our fine-tuned YOLOv8 model (cf.~\cite{Rossbach2023 ); based on the result: if no signal has been detected: ignore and do not execute any operation in the B model; if a signal has been detected:.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Correct signal (corresponding B event is enabled): execute VIS\_DetectCorrectSignal.
elements.sent-2411-076: Execute event for environment change, e.g., switch signals or activate derailer, with a probability of 25\% or move train forwards and update controller with a probability of 75\% (environment changes should occur less frequently than train movements).
elements.sent-2411-077: The simulation runs in a loop until reaching the ending condition which is later explained in our experiments.
elements.sent-2411-078: The controller of the steering system is updated after each detection to recompute the maximum allowed movement distance.
elements.sent-2411-079: Since the simple object detection AI does not provide positioning information, we place all detections in the formal model at a fixed distance in front of the train.
elements.sent-2411-080: In the second step, we (optionally) apply the certificate checker which monitors the output of the AI by accepting or rejecting its detections.
elements.sent-2411-081: For the AI to run the simulation, we use \simb{ 's interface for external simulation~\cite{vu2024validation .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: For initial experiments, we encoded a safety shield in the B model that only allows for signal detections at known positions of signals.
elements.sent-2411-083: If no signal is detected at an expected position, the B~operations for train movements are disabled so that the train falls back to safe mode and stops in front of the signal.
elements.sent-2411-084: This assumes that we have a map of the shunting yard and know at which locations the signals are located.
elements.sent-2411-085: We then analyse the behaviour using \simb{ 's real-time simulation which is now controlled by the AI and the certificate checker.}
elements.sent-2411-086: For better understanding, we use the domain-specific VisB visualisation~\cite{visb from previous work~\cite{gruteser2023formal .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Both tools are part of \probtwoui{ ~\cite{prob2ui ; an illustration is shown in Figure~\ref{fig-prob2-ui .}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{center} \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{fig/Sh1GS} \end{center} \caption{Real-time Simulation in ProB2-UI together with Domain-Specific Visualisation and corresponding image; Signal is detected correctly.} \label{fig-prob2-ui}
elements.sent-2411-088: With \simb{ , we also run Monte Carlo simulation with 500 runs for all combinations with/without safety shield, and with/without certified control.}
elements.sent-2411-089: We also investigated the effect of not applying the certificate checker to stop signals, so that these cannot be falsely rejected and the train always enters a safe state (stop).
elements.sent-2411-090: The termination condition has been defined so that a simulation stops when a safety-critical situation occurs or when the train can no longer proceed, either due to its arrival at a stop signal or reaching the end.
elements.sent-2411-091: For each execution run, we estimate the maximum (safe) distance travelled to validate that the train does move forward (not driving at all would be 100 \% safe, but not useful).
elements.sent-2411-092: Furthermore, we estimate the likelihood of a safety-critical situation where an accident might occur, similar to~\cite{gruteser2023formal , using the safety properties SAF1-5 \footnote{Note that compared to the results in~\cite{gruteser2023formal , we simulate real AI behaviour at runtime, instead of encoding fixed probabilities for how the AI, i.e., the perception system could behave.. .}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: The results are shown in Table~\ref{tab:sim-results .}
elements.sent-2411-094: With certified control, it can be obtained that all false detections are correctly rejected (in our simple environment).
elements.sent-2411-095: Unfortunately, we also observe a significant decrease in correct detections with certified control due to false rejections (as shown in the last row of Table~\ref{tab:sim-results ).}
elements.sent-2411-096: When using the safety shield, there are still many false detections, as all detections are forwarded to the controller but then ignored if they were not expected.
elements.sent-2411-097: Without safety shield and without certified control it can be observed that the train always stops early before the first wrong detected stop signal and therefore does not make progress (the train does not even arrive at a position where it could detect a signal correctly).
elements.sent-2411-098: This is because our detection model produces quite a lot of false positive detections of stop signals.
elements.sent-2411-099: As expected, these false positive detections can be effectively avoided by both the safety shield and the certificate checker.
elements.sent-2411-100: This is reflected in significantly improved values for the distance travelled in the first row of Table~\ref{tab:sim-results .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Surprisingly, the results of the combination of the safety shield and certified control are worse than those without certified control.
elements.sent-2411-102: With \prob{ , we identify the cause by inspecting the execution runs simulated in \simb{ .}}
elements.sent-2411-103: We found that falsely rejected detections by the certificate checker can still lead to safety-critical situations when a signal has been correctly detected as a permission signal but then falls back to stop.
elements.sent-2411-104: In this case, the safety shield no longer applies and a false negative detection can still cause the train to overrun the signal.
elements.sent-2411-105: Moreover, we identified particular scenarios, in which the checker encounters difficulties in certifying a correct stop signal detection, resulting in unexpectedly many false rejections~(e.g.~Fig.~\ref{fig:checking-c ).}
elements.sent-2411-106: In general, it can be observed that the likelihood of safety-critical situations is still high.
elements.sent-2411-107: This might be the case because of two reasons: (1) our custom AI model does not produce perfect results, and (2) there are still false negative detections.
elements.sent-2411-108: Our experiments revealed that the safety shield of known signal positions is still not enough to tackle these.
elements.sent-2411-109: The main benefit of our methodology is that we can link the execution of a real AI model to a formal model and check its behaviour using formal properties and statistical validation techniques.
elements.sent-2411-110: However, there are still many challenges and limitations.
elements.sent-2411-111: A major issue is to match the real environment provided to the AI with the environment's state in the formal model.
elements.sent-2411-112: This requires an interactive simulation environment with control of the environment (signals, points, etc.) and control of the train, to ensure that the actuators controlled by the formal model are taken into account by the simulated environment.
elements.sent-2411-113: We have conducted some successful experiments with a commercial train simulator, but since it is not designed for discrete states, as required by the formal model, the handling is complicated (apart from the fact that a new instance of the simulator would have to be set up for each simulation run).
elements.sent-2411-114: Simply using a video as input is not sufficient either, as the environment remains static and we have no control over the movement of the train.
elements.sent-2411-115: In our current experiments, we avoid this problem by sampling images from videos fitting to the current state.
elements.sent-2411-116: Although this is sufficient to demonstrate the concept, we have not yet simulated real train rides.
elements.sent-2411-117: This requires a simulation environment for configurable scenarios (with \prob{ we are already able to load and visualise flexible scenarios, e.g., via a standardised data exchange format such as railML~\cite{gruteser-railml ).}}
elements.sent-2411-118: While there are already established tools in the automotive sector, e.g., CARLA~\cite{carla , such tools are still rare in the railway sector, but are under development~\cite{trainsimdataset,fraunhoferSimEnv,wild2023towards .}}
elements.sent-2411-119: There are many approaches to verifying neural networks~\cite{katz2017reluplex,ruan2018reachability,verification-dnn .}
elements.sent-2411-120: In practical application, however, it is challenging for these techniques to scale to large neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-121: Another technique is robustness checks~\cite{gehr2018ai2,robustness which also work on neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Robustness checks aim to ensure the safety of the AI directly, while this work employs safety boxes around the AI.
elements.sent-2411-123: For instance, the perception system could be unsafe, but is monitored by a certificate checker.
elements.sent-2411-124: Similarly, the steering system could make unsafe decisions based on the perception, but is monitored by a safety shield encoded in the formal model.
elements.sent-2411-125: Another work presented by Pasareanu et al.~\cite{perception-DNN abstracts away perception components, and replaces them with a probabilistic component that estimates their behaviour.}
elements.sent-2411-126: In particular, the probabilities are derived from confusion matrices computed for the underlying neural network.
elements.sent-2411-127: Finally, the verification is done by probabilistic model checkers such as PRISM~\cite{kwiatkowska2011prism and STORM~\cite{hensel2022probabilistic .}}
elements.sent-2411-128: To improve safety, Pasareanu et al.~\cite{perception-DNN employ run-time guards which are used as runtime monitors.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Instead of estimating the probabilities for real AI behaviour, this work simulates real AI behaviour at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-130: This means that the perception system operates at runtime with real images and provides the detection to the validation tools \prob{ and \simb{ .}}
elements.sent-2411-131: Alternatively, we could have extracted confusion matrices and encoded them as probabilities into the simulation.
elements.sent-2411-132: This work successfully links a formal model, AI, and certified control to a real-time demonstrator.
elements.sent-2411-133: We demonstrated the technique in an AI-based train system.
elements.sent-2411-134: The methodology consists of the following steps: (1) formally specify and verify the steering system using formal methods, (2) encode safety shields in the formal model to prevent unsafe operations, (3) use real AI for simulation, (4) add a runtime certificate checker of the AI outputs to reduce false positive detections, and (5) link all components to simulate the formal model with the AI and the certificate checker's output.
elements.sent-2411-135: Using the tools \prob{ and \simb{ , we then evaluate the performance of the AI with (and without) certified control and safety shield.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: With \simb{ , we can make statistical statements about the formal properties of the whole system.}
elements.sent-2411-137: We analysed the likelihood of unsafe situations and identified weaknesses in our AI and certificate checker.
elements.sent-2411-138: With our approach, we can identify issues early during development.
elements.sent-2411-139: The results are then used to improve the AI or the certificate checker, followed by further validation.
elements.sent-2411-140: In our case study, we identified false negatives of certified control as a cause of unsafe situations.
elements.sent-2411-141: In future, we thus need to improve our safety shield, to better protect against such false negative detections and reduce the error rates to levels required for certification.
elements.sent-2411-142: Other future improvements are to link our tool with a realistic simulation environment, e.g., in the form of co-simulation.
elements.sent-2411-143: We thank Hitachi for providing the case study and parts of the video material.. \bibliographystyle{eptcs.}
